claimSummary: Providence Water tells customers to use the lead service location map to learn whether the utility side or private side is lead or unknown at a property.
reviewerNote: Official Providence Water lead map page reviewed manually for map and public-private material split.

claimSummary: Providence Water says customers can check the map for lead or unknown lines, annual notices go to affected addresses, and eligible properties can sign up for replacement through the current construction queue instead of the separate loan path.
reviewerNote: Official Providence Water replacement program page reviewed manually for annual notice and eligibility workflow.

claimSummary: Providence Water says it is using federal and state funding to replace lead service lines for free in planned contracts and aims to replace all lead service lines in its service area by 2033.
reviewerNote: Official Providence Water replacement program page reviewed manually for free planned-contract replacement and 2033 target.

claimSummary: Providence Water says owners outside planned contracts can use a 10-year 0 percent interest loan and that the utility will replace the public side at no cost only when the private side is replaced through that separate loan path.
reviewerNote: Official Providence Water replacement program page reviewed manually for 10-year 0 percent loan and public-side coverage rules.

claimSummary: Philadelphia Water Department says its service line map categorizes properties as lead non-lead galvanized metal incomplete records or mixed based on available records and verification points.
reviewerNote: Official Philadelphia service line map page reviewed manually for current map categories and inventory wording.

claimSummary: Philadelphia Water Department says letters mailed in November 2024 and December 2025 provided information about customer-owned service line materials at every property.
reviewerNote: Official Philadelphia notification explainer reviewed manually for the 2024 and 2025 mailed notice language.

claimSummary: Philadelphia's Service Line Verification Program is a limited free program and if lead or galvanized metal is found the service line is replaced for free at no cost to the property owner only in that selected verification path.
reviewerNote: Official Philadelphia verification program page reviewed manually for limited free verification and no-cost replacement rules.

claimSummary: Philadelphia Water says HELP is a zero-interest loan and the lead service line version can be used when a property has a confirmed lead service line outside the limited free verification path.
reviewerNote: Official Philadelphia HELP loan page reviewed manually for the confirmed lead service line eligibility path.

claimSummary: Pittsburgh Water says Community Lead Response is on track to replace each lead line in Pittsburgh by 2027 and that unknown service line locations continue to be improved through neighborhood lead line work and water main replacements.
reviewerNote: Official Pittsburgh Water lead information page reviewed manually for 2027 replacement timing and unknown-line cleanup language.

claimSummary: Pittsburgh Water tells customers that residential customers who have a lead service line will have it replaced at no direct cost and links affected owners to the reimbursement program for private-side work.
reviewerNote: Official Pittsburgh Water lead information page reviewed manually for no-direct-cost replacement language and reimbursement referrals.

claimSummary: Pittsburgh Water says the reimbursement program may reimburse up to 100 percent of private lead line replacement costs depending on income and includes a 1000 dollar stipend tier for higher incomes after owners complete the work and submit documentation.
reviewerNote: Official Pittsburgh Water reimbursement page reviewed manually for income-based reimbursement and stipend tiers.
